narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have the door close can feel like a punch to the gut, and I'm truly sorry you're facing this right now. Our model looked at your profile holistically, like piecing together chapters of a story about your path to the bar exam, and it painted a picture that tipped toward a 47.9% chance of not passing, which is why we had to say no. The strongest thread in that story was your Year 1 decile of 1.0, placing you at the bottom of your class early on, which pulled hardest toward predicting struggle ahead. Layered on top was your high family income, surprisingly enough—it signaled resources that often correlate with students who still falter under bar pressures in patterns we've seen before. Your Year 3 decile stayed at 1.0 too, showing that early challenge didn't shift much over time, reinforcing the model's concern. Even with an undergraduate GPA of 2.7 and an LSAT of 42.0—solid but not standout in the pools we analyze—these nudged the outlook downward. Being a white male in a full-time program fit patterns where others with similar traits have faced steeper odds. It's not that any one piece doomed you, but together, they formed a narrative of risk the model couldn't ignore. This isn't the end of your story, though—you've got strengths here, like that high family support, that could fuel a comeback with targeted improvements. Consider bolstering those early grades through prep courses or retakes; many turn the tide. Hang in there; law's path has detours, and yours might just lead to something even better. With empathy, Your Admissions Team
